content.content:
AI in Transportation Planning and Engineering: 
From Chatbots to Workflow Intelligence
A practical overview for AI Development, Use Cases, Risks, Governance, and Future Direction

notes: Expanded content review draft. No section labels or slide numbers are shown.

content.content:
What This Session Covers?
Five practical questions about AI and our daily project work

Why is everyone talking about AI now?A quick look at how AI changed in the last two years and why it matters for transportation planning and engineering.
What changed from chatbot to agent?How AI moved from simple Q&A to tools, skills, agents, and digital project-team workflows.
Can we trust AI’s answers?Why AI sometimes gets things wrong, why hallucination happens, and where human review is still required.
How can we actually use AI in our work?Practical examples for literature review, document comparison, GIS/data workflows, modeling support, dashboards, RAG, and NotebookLM.
What should we be careful about?Basic rules for government-client work, confidential data, professional responsibility, job impact, and future opportunities.
EXPANDED CONTENT DRAFT • content first; structure and design later
Set expectation: this is a practical overview, not a technical deep dive

## Page 1 — Title / Opening and Section Overview
[Original heading preserved: AI Overview for Transportation Planning and Engineering / Proposed PPT Outline / Slide 1 — Title / Opening]

AI Overview for Transportation Planning and Engineering
Proposed PPT Outline

Slide 1 — Title / Opening
AI in Transportation Planning and Engineering
Development, Use Cases, Risks, and Future Direction
Subtitle:
A practical overview for transportation planners and engineers
Purpose:
Introduce the topic
Set expectation: this is a practical overview, not a technical deep dive
Audience: transportation planners, traffic engineers, modelers, GIS/data analysts, project managers
Opening message:
AI is no longer just a chatbot for summarizing text. It is becoming a workflow tool that can support research, writing, coding, GIS, data analysis, QA/QC, knowledge management, and project delivery. The key question is not whether AI is useful, but how to use it safely, effectively, and responsibly in transportation work.

### Section 1 — Why AI Matters Now
[Updated page range: Pages 2–3]
Slides 2–3
Focus: past two years of rapid AI development; why planners and engineers should care.

### Section 2 — From Chatbot to Agentic Workflow
[Updated page range: Pages 4–7]
Slides 4–7
Focus: chatbot → assistant → agent → skills → multi-agent collaboration; use the “digital project team” story.

### Section 3 — What AI Can and Cannot Do
[Updated page range: Pages 8–10]
Slides 8–10
Focus: strengths, limitations, hallucination, “Can I trust AI?”

### Section 4 — Transportation Planning / Engineering Use Cases
[Updated page range: Pages 11–14 and Page 17]
Slides 11–14
Focus: documents, reports, GIS, Python, modeling, dashboards, public comments, project delivery.

### Section 5 — RAG and NotebookLM Demo

Slides 15–16
Focus: why PDF summaries fail; RAG logic; NotebookLM demo with 23 State DOT RIP PDFs.

### Section 6 — Governance, Risks, and Future Direction
[Updated page range: Pages 18–24]
Slides 17–20
Focus: federal/state AI rules, client data, government projects, human review, future trends, final takeaway.

content.content: Why AI Matters Now
EXPANDED CONTENT DRAFT • content first; structure and design later

Why AI Matters Now
AI has moved from experimental chatbot to practical workflow support.
What changed:• Writing, coding, research, meeting notes, and data analysis• PDFs, spreadsheets, images, code, and project documents• From “Can AI help?” to “How do we use it safely?”
Why it matters to us:• Transportation work is document-heavy and data-heavy• Projects require regulations, assumptions, QA/QC, and coordination• AI can help us organize, review, summarize, and communicate faster
Key message:AI is becoming part of the workflow, not just a tool for summarizing text.

notes:
Source: ai_ppt_full_content_dump_numbered.md — Page 2

## Page 2 — Why This Matters Now
[Original label: Slide 2 — Why This Matters Now]
AI moved from experimental to operational very quickly
Main points:
In the past two years, AI capability improved dramatically
AI is now used for writing, coding, data analysis, research, GIS workflows, meeting notes, and knowledge management
The question is no longer “Can AI be useful?” but “Where can we use it safely and effectively?”
Transportation angle:
Our industry is document-heavy, data-heavy, regulation-heavy, and coordination-heavy
That makes transportation planning and engineering a strong fit for AI-assisted workflows

---

content.content: AI Timeline: From ChatGPT to Frontier Models

Main message:
The last two years of AI progress were not gradual. Each major jump was driven by a stronger foundation model, then quickly turned into new tools, products, and workflows.
AI model milestones timeline:
2022/11 — OpenAI ChatGPT / GPT-3.5
The public “chatbot moment.” AI became accessible to regular users.

2023/03 — OpenAI GPT-4
A major jump in reasoning, writing, coding, and professional use.

2024/02 — Google Gemini 1.5 Pro
Long-context and multimodal capability became a major competition area.

2024/03 — Anthropic Claude 3
Claude became a serious frontier-model competitor for writing, reasoning, coding, and document work.

2024/05 — OpenAI GPT-4o
AI moved toward real-time multimodal interaction: text, vision, and audio together.

2024/06 — Anthropic Claude 3.5 Sonnet
Claude became especially strong in coding, writing, and long-context document tasks.

2024/09 — OpenAI o1-preview / reasoning models
Models started spending more compute on “thinking” before answering complex questions.
EXPANDED CONTENT DRAFT • content first; structure and design later

notes:
Source: ai_ppt_full_content_dump_numbered.md — Page 3

## Page 3 — AI Timeline: From ChatGPT to Frontier Models
[Original label: Slide 3 — AI Timeline: From ChatGPT to Frontier Models]

Main message:
The last two years of AI progress were not gradual. Each major jump was driven by a stronger foundation model, then quickly turned into new tools, products, and workflows.

Simple timeline:

2022 — OpenAI ChatGPT / GPT-3.5
The public “chatbot moment.” AI became accessible to regular users.

2023 — OpenAI GPT-4
A major jump in reasoning, writing, coding, and professional use.

2024 — Google Gemini 1.5 Pro
Long-context and multimodal capability became a major competition area.

2024 — Anthropic Claude 3 / Claude 3.5 Sonnet
Claude became especially strong in writing, coding, document work, and long-context tasks.

2024 — OpenAI GPT-4o
AI moved toward real-time multimodal interaction: text, vision, and audio together.

2024–2025 — OpenAI o1 / reasoning models
Models started spending more compute on “thinking” before answering complex questions.

2025 — DeepSeek-R1
Open-source reasoning models became a serious competitive force.

2025 — Anthropic Claude 4
The focus shifted strongly toward coding, advanced reasoning, and AI agents.

2025 — OpenAI GPT-5
AI systems became more focused on coding, agentic tasks, and longer tool-use workflows.

2026 — OpenAI GPT-5.5 / Anthropic Claude Fable 5 & Mythos 5
The frontier moved toward stronger agentic work, coding, cybersecurity-sensitive capability, and government-level access control.

Fun fact / current event:
In June 2026, the U.S. government issued an export-control directive requiring Anthropic to suspend access to Claude Fable 5 and Claude Mythos 5 for foreign nationals, including Anthropic’s own foreign-national employees. Anthropic said it had to disable access broadly to comply. This is a useful example showing that frontier AI models are now being treated not just as software products, but as strategic technology with national-security implications.

Speaker note:
Don’t make this slide too technical. The key story is that AI development is model-driven: every major model release expands what people can do. First it was chatbot Q&A. Then it became document reading, coding, voice, images, reasoning, tool use, agents, and now regulated frontier capability.

---

content.content: Chatbot vs. AI Agent

AI is shifting from simple conversation to workflow execution.
A chatbot answers individual questions. It is useful for quick summaries, explanations, writing support, translation, and brainstorming. But the user still has to manage the full workflow.
An AI agent works toward a broader goal. It can break the task into steps, use tools, apply assigned skills, check intermediate results, and produce a more complete output.
Example:Chatbot: “Summarize this resilience guidance document.”
Agent: “Review these State DOT resilience plans, compare risk scoring methods, build a table, draft a memo outline, and flag missing information.”

Tools are what the agent can use: PDFs, spreadsheets, Python, GIS, databases, web search, and document generation.
Skills are reusable playbooks: PDF review, data QA/QC, methodology comparison, dashboard requirements, meeting summary, and slide preparation.
Key takeaway:A chatbot responds. An agent organizes and executes a workflow.
EXPANDED CONTENT DRAFT • content first; structure and design later

notes:
Source: ai_ppt_full_content_dump_numbered.md — Page 6

## Page 6 — Agent, Tools, and Skills
[Original label: Slide 3C — Agent, Tools, and Skills]
Skills are reusable capabilities that help agents perform specific types of work
Main points:
A tool is something the AI can call
A skill is a packaged workflow or specialized capability
An agent uses tools and skills to complete a larger task
Simple distinction:
Tool:
“Run Python”
“Search documents”
“Read a spreadsheet”
“Generate slides”
“Query a database”
Skill:
“Analyze a PDF report”
“Create a PowerPoint deck”
“Summarize meeting notes”
“Build a data QA/QC checklist”
“Convert raw project notes into a structured user story”
Agent:
Decides when and how to use these tools and skills to complete a goal
Transportation example:
Task: “Create a project risk dashboard concept”
Skills involved:
Summarize project guidance
Extract scoring categories
Clean asset data
Design filters
Draft dashboard user stories
Prepare a stakeholder-facing explanation
Key message:
Skills make AI more repeatable. Instead of prompting from scratch every time, a team can define standard workflows.
Visual suggestion:
Hierarchy diagram:
Goal
Agent
Skills
Tools
Data / documents / code / maps
Speaker note:
In our industry, this is important because we do not only need creative answers. We need repeatable, auditable, project-specific workflows.
---

content.content:
A transportation planning task example:
Before and After using AI Agents

User case senario:
Imagine we are preparing a resilience planning task for a DOT.

Without AI, A traditional literature review can take weeks or months, reading hundreds or thousands pages document depending on the number of source documents, required synthesis, and deliverable format.

In the old chatbot mode, we ask:
“Summarize this resilience guidance document.”
The chatbot gives us a summary, which is helpful. But most of the actual work still remains with the human team: opening every PDF, comparing different plans, building tables, writing code, drafting slides, and checking consistency.

”

In the agent mode, we give a broader goal:
“Help me prepare an internal briefing on how different State DOTs evaluate transportation resilience risk.”
The agent does not just answer one question. It starts organizing the workflow: reading the uploaded guidance documents, identifying major risk categories, extracting scoring methods, comparing base-year, mid-term, and long-term scenarios, creating comparison tables, flagging missing information, drafting a technical memo outline, generating slide content, and suggesting QA/QC checks before the output is used.
EXPANDED CONTENT DRAFT • content first; structure and design later

notes:
Source: ai_ppt_full_content_dump_numbered.md — Page 7

## Page 7 — A Simple Story: Planning Office Before and After Agents
[Original label: Slide 3D — A Simple Story: Planning Office Before and After Agents]
A transportation planning example
Story:
Imagine we are preparing a resilience planning task for a DOT.
In the old chatbot mode, we ask:
“Summarize this resilience guidance document.”
The chatbot gives a summary. That is helpful, but the work still depends on us to open every PDF, compare every plan, build tables, write code, draft slides, and check consistency.
In the agent mode, we give a broader goal:
“Help me prepare an internal briefing on how different state DOTs evaluate transportation resilience risk.”
The agent does not just answer one question. It starts organizing the work:
It reads the uploaded guidance documents
It identifies the main risk categories
It extracts scoring methods
It compares base year, mid-term, and long-term scenarios
It creates a table of similarities and differences
It flags missing information
It drafts a technical memo outline
It generates slide content for a project meeting
It suggests QA/QC checks before the result is used
Now imagine a higher-level multi-agent workflow:
Research Agent:
Reviews DOT reports, federal guidance, and academic references
Data Agent:
Checks asset data, risk scores, TAZ-level outputs, VMT/VHT, and scenario tables
GIS Agent:
Designs map layers, filters, heat maps, and dashboard logic
Writing Agent:
Drafts memo language, slide text, and stakeholder summaries
QA/QC Agent:
Checks assumptions, missing fields, inconsistent numbers, and unsupported claims
Human Lead:
Reviews the output, applies professional judgment, decides what is acceptable for the client deliverable
Key message:
Multi-agent collaboration does not mean AI replaces the project team.
It means AI can imitate parts of a project workflow, while humans remain responsible for judgment, quality, and final decisions.
Visual suggestion:
Use a “project team” diagram:
Human Project Lead in the center
Around it:
Research Agent
Data Agent
GIS Agent
Writing Agent
QA/QC Agent
Final output:
Briefing memo
Dashboard concept
Risk methodology comparison
Presentation slides
Speaker note:
This story helps people understand that agentic AI is not one super-intelligent robot. It is closer to a coordinated digital project team, where each agent has a narrow role and the human remains the project manager.

---

content.content: Planning Office Before and After Agents — Continued

GIS Agent: Supports map-based analysis by organizing layers, suggesting filters, designing heat maps, and preparing dashboard logic for spatial risk communication.
Writing Agent: Drafts technical memo language, slide content, executive summaries, stakeholder-facing explanations, and meeting materials.
QA/QC Agent: Reviews assumptions, checks numbers, flags unsupported claims, compares outputs against source documents, and identifies inconsistencies before the material is used.
Human Lead: Defines the project objective, reviews the AI outputs, applies professional judgment, communicates with the client, and remains responsible for the final deliverable.

Now imagine a higher-level multi-agent workflow. Instead of asking one AI assistant to do everything, different specialized agents support different parts of the project.
Research Agent: Reviews State DOT reports, federal guidance, academic references, and prior project materials. It identifies relevant policies, methodologies, best practices, and common themes.
Data Agent:Checks asset data, risk scores, scenario tables, TAZ-level outputs, VMT/VHT summaries, and other project datasets. It helps identify missing fields, inconsistent values, and data quality issues.

content.content: How Human-in-the-Loop AI Works Like a Digital Project Team

Agentic AI works like a digital project team: agents perform specialized tasks, skills define how they work, tools provide capabilities, and the human lead remains responsible for direction, judgment, and final approval.

Human Lead as Project Manager The human defines the objective, assigns tasks, sets quality standards, reviews outputs, makes judgment calls, and remains responsible for the final deliverable.
Agents as Specialized Team Members Each agent supports a specific part of the workflow, such as research, data review, GIS analysis, writing, or QA/QC.
Skills as Assigned Capabilities Skills are reusable playbooks or capabilities assigned to agents, such as document review, data cleaning, methodology comparison, dashboard design, or slide preparation.
Tools and Data as Work Resources Agents use tools and data sources to complete their assigned tasks, including PDFs, spreadsheets, GIS layers, Python scripts, databases, and internal knowledge bases.
Escalation to Human When the agents encounter missing information, conflicting assumptions, uncertain results, or high-risk decisions, they escalate the issue to the human lead for direction.

content.content: What AI Is Actually Good At and Not Good At?

AI is best at language, structure, pattern recognition, and workflow acceleration
Good uses:
Summarizing long documents
Drafting emails, reports, meeting notes, and proposals
Explaining technical concepts
Creating outlines and presentation materials
Writing and debugging code
Transforming messy notes into structured content
Extracting themes from documents
Helping design workflows
Important limitation:
AI does not automatically know what is true
It can sound confident while being wrong

AI is not a replacement for professional judgment
Limitations:
AI may:
• Generate false but plausible information, including facts, citations, numbers, or policies• Miss highly specific local context unless that context is provided• Struggle with proprietary or internal data unless securely connected• Misread maps, tables, engineering assumptions, or model outputs• Produce analysis that sounds reasonable but is technically incorrect• Be unreliable as the sole basis for legal, engineering, safety, or funding decisions
Key Message
AI should support professional judgment, not replace it.
EXPANDED CONTENT DRAFT • content first; structure and design later

content.content: Why Does AI Sometimes “Lie”? Can I Trust AI?

Why Does AI Sometimes “Lie”? Can We Trust AI?
AI does not usually “lie” in the human sense. It is not trying to deceive the user.
Instead, AI generates the most likely response based on language patterns, available context, and the prompt. This means it can produce answers that sound fluent and confident, even when they are wrong. This is called a hallucination (huh-loo-suh-NAY-shun) .
Common causes:
• Not enough context• Misunderstanding the question• Missing tables, maps, figures, footnotes, or appendices• Difficulty reading long, scanned, or poorly formatted documents• Mixing uploaded information with general training knowledge• Producing plausible but unsupported analysis
EXPANDED CONTENT DRAFT • content first; structure and design later

Better question:
Not just: Can I trust AI?But: Can I verify the answer from a reliable source?
Key Message
AI is useful for brainstorming, drafting, summarizing, and organizing information.
For engineering, legal, safety, funding, or client-facing decisions, AI output must be checked by a human professional.
Trust AI as an assistant, not as an authority.

notes:
Source: ai_ppt_full_content_dump_numbered.md — Page 10

## Page 10 — Why Does AI Sometimes “Lie”? Can I Trust AI?
[Original label: Why Does AI Sometimes “Lie”? Can I Trust AI?]
内容可以这样写：
AI does not usually “lie” in the human sense. It is not intentionally trying to deceive the user. The real issue is that AI generates the most likely answer based on patterns in language, available context, and the user’s prompt.
That means AI can produce an answer that sounds fluent and confident, even when the answer is wrong. This is often called a hallucination.
This can happen for several reasons. The AI may not have enough context. It may misunderstand the question. It may fail to read a long PDF correctly. It may miss tables, figures, scanned pages, footnotes, or appendices. It may also mix information from the uploaded document with its general training knowledge.
So the better question is not simply “Can I trust AI?” The better question is: “Is this AI answer grounded in a reliable source, and can I verify it?”
For low-risk tasks like brainstorming, outlining, rewriting, or summarizing meeting notes, AI can be very useful. For high-risk tasks like engineering conclusions, legal interpretation, official numbers, safety decisions, or client deliverables, AI output must be checked by a human professional.
Key message:
AI can be trusted as an assistant, not as an authority. Trust the workflow, the sources, and the verification process — not just the confident wording.

---

content.content:
RAG and Knowledge Bases
A better way to use AI with technical documents

How RAG Works — Simple Explanation
RAG means Retrieval-Augmented Generation.
In simple terms, RAG works in two steps:
Step 1: RetrieveThe system searches the selected documents and finds the most relevant sections.
Step 2: GenerateThe AI uses those retrieved sections to generate an answer.
The key idea is that the AI is not just answering from memory. It is answering based on the documents we provide.
Behind the scenes, the system converts text into vectors, which are like “meaning coordinates.” Text with similar meaning is placed closer together, even if the exact words are different.
EXPANDED CONTENT DRAFT • content first; structure and design later

Simple Example
User asks:Which DOT plans discuss flooding risk?
The document may use different terms, such as:
• Flooding• Sea level rise• Storm surge• Coastal inundation• Drainage impacts• Riverine flooding
RAG can find these related sections because they are semantically similar, not only because they share the exact same keyword.
Key Message
RAG helps AI search the right documents first, then answer based on the most relevant source material.

notes:
Source: ai_ppt_full_content_dump_numbered.md — Page 13

## Page 13 — RAG and Knowledge Bases
[Original label: Slide 8 — RAG and Knowledge Bases]
The better way to use AI with technical documents
Definition:
RAG means Retrieval-Augmented Generation
The AI answers based on a selected document set instead of relying only on its general training knowledge
Why it matters:
Better for agency manuals, project reports, guidance documents, meeting notes, and technical standards
Reduces hallucination
Makes answers more traceable
Helps teams reuse institutional knowledge
Example:
Upload state DOT resilience plans, PROTECT guidance, risk assessment reports, and model documentation
Ask the AI to compare methods, summarize assumptions, or find specific criteria
Key message:
For our work, AI is most valuable when connected to the right documents and data.

---

content.content: RAG / NotebookLM Demo: 23 State DOT RIP PDFsUsing AI to compare resilience methods across technical documents

Tool: NotebookLM
Developer: Google
Access: Web-based tool with a free version and usage limits
Practical Benefit: Accessible from our company laptops through a browser, making it easy to demonstrate RAG-style document search without custom software development.
Source Library: 23 State DOT Resilience Improvement Plan / resilience-related PDF documents
Example Questions

# Slide 17 — NotebookLM Demo: 23 State DOT RIP PDFs

## Title
NotebookLM Demo: 23 State DOT RIP PDFs

## Main message
NotebookLM is a simple RAG-style example for technical document search and synthesis.

## Demo setup
- Tool: NotebookLM
- Source library: 23 State DOT Resilience Improvement Plan / resilience-related PDF documents
- Demo link: https://notebooklm.google.com/notebook/170159b2-1d20-4a3c-91f3-1afbe9e63658?pli=1

## Demo purpose
The goal is not just to summarize PDFs. The goal is to compare methods, extract assumptions, identify gaps, and support project-ready synthesis.

## Example questions
- What hazards are commonly included across these DOT plans?
- Which states use risk scoring?
- How do different DOTs define vulnerability, criticality, exposure, and equity?
- Do the plans discuss base year, mid-term, or long-term scenarios?
- Can we create a methodology comparison table by state?
- What assumptions are unclear?
- What are the common gaps?

## Key message
For transportation planning, this is close to how we actually work: compare guidance documents, extract methodologies, check assumptions, and turn complex information into memos, slides, dashboard requirements, or project recommendations.

## Visual suggestion
Show a mock “source library” on the left and “question / synthesis / citations” on the right.

## Speaker notes
Emphasize that NotebookLM is not the only RAG tool, but it is a simple and easy-to-understand demo. It shows the basic value of grounding AI in a controlled source set.

content.content: NotebookLM Tips: What Else Can It Be Used For?

Good Use Cases
• Software manualsFind steps, troubleshoot errors, compare workflows, and create quick user guides.
• Traffic design manualsSearch design criteria, summarize requirements, compare chapters, and identify applicable standards.
• HCM / technical methodsFind definitions, methodology steps, assumptions, input requirements, and calculation procedures.
• Planning codes and policiesSummarize requirements, compare policy language, extract review criteria, and identify approval conditions.
• Agency guidance documentsCompare DOT guidance, federal guidance, local procedures, and project-specific requirements.
• Meeting notes and project recordsSummarize decisions, action items, assumptions, open questions, and follow-up needs.
• Model documentationFind calibration assumptions, scenario definitions, data sources, limitations, and methodology notes.
EXPANDED CONTENT DRAFT • content first; structure and design later

Practical Tips
• Create one notebook per project or topic• Upload only the documents relevant to that question• Ask for answers with source citations• Ask the AI to create comparison tables• Ask what assumptions are unclear or missing• Use it to draft memos, slides, checklists, and dashboard requirements• Always verify important outputs against the original source documents
Key Message
NotebookLM is not just for summarizing documents. It can help turn manuals, standards, reports, codes, and project notes into a searchable knowledge base.
NotebookLM is useful when we need to search, compare, and synthesize technical documents.

content.content: Common Concerns, Guardrails, and Professional Responsibility

Common Concerns, Guardrails, and Professional Responsibility
AI concerns are valid, but they should be managed with guardrails — not ignored.
Common Concerns
• Will AI replace planners or engineers?• Can we trust the answer?• Is it allowed for client work?• What happens to confidential data?• Will it create legal or professional risk?
Practical Guardrails
• Do not upload confidential client data to unauthorized tools• Do not treat AI output as final• Verify sources, numbers, citations, assumptions, and policy references• Use human review for client deliverables and high-risk decisions• Follow company, client, and agency AI policies• Document AI-assisted workflows when needed
Balanced Answer
AI can improve productivity, but weak review creates risk.
The risk is not using AI; the risk is using AI casually without controls.
Key Message
AI supports the professional team. It does not replace professional judgment or responsibility.
EXPANDED CONTENT DRAFT • content first; structure and design later

notes:
Source: ai_ppt_full_content_dump_numbered.md — Page 14

## Page 14 — Legal, Policy, and Compliance Considerations
[Original label: Slide 9 — Legal, Policy, and Compliance Considerations]
AI use needs guardrails
Topics:
Confidentiality
Client data protection
Public records implications
Copyright and licensing
Data privacy
Sensitive infrastructure information
Professional liability
Use of AI-generated content in deliverables
Need for human review and documentation
Practical guidance:
Do not upload confidential client data to unauthorized tools
Do not treat AI output as final
Verify sources, numbers, citations, and assumptions
Follow company, client, and agency AI policies
Keep a record of AI-assisted workflows when needed

---

content.content: Government-Client Projects: Rules and Guardrails

Case 1 — Federal AI Use Policy: OMB M-25-21
Federal agencies are encouraged to adopt AI, but they must manage it through governance, risk management, transparency, and public trust. 
OMB M-25-21 tells agencies to accelerate AI use while maintaining safeguards for privacy, civil rights, civil liberties, and high-impact AI use cases.

Source: White House / OMB, “M-25-21: Accelerating Federal Use of AI through Innovation, Governance, and Public Trust.”

Case 2 — Federal AI Procurement: OMB M-25-22
When federal agencies buy AI systems or AI services, they need to consider performance, risk, competition, vendor lock-in, government data rights, privacy, cybersecurity, and cross-functional review. 
This matters to consultants because AI-related tools or deliverables may be treated as part of federal technology acquisition or government data use.

Source: White House / OMB, “M-25-22: Driving Efficient Acquisition of Artificial Intelligence in Government.”
EXPANDED CONTENT DRAFT • content first; structure and design later
AI is not generally banned in government transportation work, but it is increasingly controlled by agency policy, procurement rules, data protection rules, and human-review requirements.

notes:
Source: ai_ppt_full_content_dump_numbered.md — Page 18

## Page 18 — AI Use for Government-Client Projects: Current Rules and Guardrails
[Original label: Slide — AI Use for Government-Client Projects: Current Rules and Guardrails]

AI is not generally banned in government transportation work, but it is increasingly controlled by agency policy, procurement rules, data protection rules, and human-review requirements.

Case 1 — Federal AI Use Policy: OMB M-25-21

Federal agencies are encouraged to adopt AI, but they must manage it through governance, risk management, transparency, and public trust. OMB M-25-21 tells agencies to accelerate AI use while maintaining safeguards for privacy, civil rights, civil liberties, and high-impact AI use cases.

Source: White House / OMB, “M-25-21: Accelerating Federal Use of AI through Innovation, Governance, and Public Trust.”

Case 2 — Federal AI Procurement: OMB M-25-22

When federal agencies buy AI systems or AI services, they need to consider performance, risk, competition, vendor lock-in, government data rights, privacy, cybersecurity, and cross-functional review. This matters to consultants because AI-related tools or deliverables may be treated as part of federal technology acquisition or government data use.

Source: White House / OMB, “M-25-22: Driving Efficient Acquisition of Artificial Intelligence in Government.”

Case 3 — U.S. DOT AI Direction

U.S. DOT supports AI for transportation safety, innovation, automated systems, traffic management, internal operations, research, citizen-facing services, NLP, computer vision, and predictive analytics. U.S. DOT also links its AI strategy to OMB M-25-21 compliance planning.

Source: U.S. DOT, “Artificial Intelligence Activities.”

Case 4 — FHWA AI Deployment Center of Excellence

FHWA created the AI Deployment Center of Excellence as a national resource to guide AI adoption across surface transportation. The focus is not “use AI casually”; it is coordinated adoption, best practices, lower deployment risk, workforce development, and AI use cases that improve planning, operations, maintenance, and investment decisions.

Source: FHWA, “Artificial Intelligence Deployment Center of Excellence.”

Case 5 — Georgia State Government / GTA GenAI Policy

Georgia Technology Authority has a statewide Generative AI Responsible Use policy, effective July 1, 2025, covering responsible use, development, procurement, and governance of GenAI within Georgia executive branch agencies. The Georgia guidelines also say state employees should use pre-vetted tools, record prompts, review AI-generated content, and keep a human-in-the-loop approach.

Source: Georgia Technology Authority, “Generative AI Responsible Use SS-25-001.”
Source: Georgia Technology Authority, “Generative AI Guidelines for Responsible Use GS-25-001.”

Case 6 — GDOT AI Policy Statement

I did not find a publicly posted standalone GDOT AI policy document. What I found is an AASHTO article quoting GDOT leadership. According to AASHTO, GDOT has an AI policy statement focused on data governance, risk, and a framework for evaluating new AI projects. That is useful to mention, but I would label it as “reported by AASHTO,” not as a formal GDOT source unless you obtain the internal GDOT document.

Source: AASHTO Journal, “AASHTO Survey Reviews Impact of AI on Operations.”

Case 7 — Other State DOT Example: MnDOT

MnDOT has a clear GenAI standard that applies to employees and third parties working on behalf of MnDOT. It says private, confidential, protected, or otherwise not public data must not be entered into public GenAI tools. It also says GenAI should be supplemental, not a replacement for human judgment, and AI output used for decision-making must be verified for accuracy, relevance, and factuality.

Source: Minnesota DOT, “Generative Artificial Intelligence — Standards.”

Case 8 — Risk Management Framework: NIST AI RMF

NIST AI Risk Management Framework is not a transportation-specific regulation, and it is voluntary, but it is a major federal reference framework for managing AI risk. It is useful when explaining why project teams should think in terms of trustworthiness, governance, risk mapping, measurement, and monitoring.

Source: NIST, “AI Risk Management Framework.”

Practical takeaway for consultants

Use only client-approved and company-approved AI tools. Do not upload confidential, sensitive, security-related, PII, or nonpublic client data into public AI systems. Keep humans in the loop. Verify AI outputs, citations, numbers, assumptions, and sources. For government-client deliverables, treat AI as drafting, research, QA/QC, and workflow support — not as the final authority.

---

U.S. DOT also links its AI strategy to OMB M-25-21 compliance planning.
Source: U.S. DOT, “Artificial Intelligence Activities.”

Source: FHWA, “Artificial Intelligence Deployment Center of Excellence.”

Case 5 — Georgia State Government / GTA GenAI Policy

Georgia Technology Authority has a statewide Generative AI Responsible Use policy, effective July 1, 2025, covering responsible use, development, procurement, and governance of GenAI within Georgia executive branch agencies. The Georgia guidelines also say state employees should use pre-vetted tools, record prompts, review AI-generated content, and keep a human-in-the-loop approach.

Source: Georgia Technology Authority, “Generative AI Responsible Use SS-25-001.”
Source: Georgia Technology Authority, “Generative AI Guidelines for Responsible Use GS-25-001.”

Case 6 — GDOT AI Policy Statement

I did not find a publicly posted standalone GDOT AI policy document. What I found is an AASHTO article quoting GDOT leadership. According to AASHTO, GDOT has an AI policy statement focused on data governance, risk, and a framework for evaluating new AI projects. That is useful to mention, but I would label it as “reported by AASHTO,” not as a formal GDOT source unless you obtain the internal GDOT document.

Source: AASHTO Journal, “AASHTO Survey Reviews Impact of AI on Operations.”

Case 7 — Other State DOT Example: MnDOT

MnDOT has a clear GenAI standard that applies to employees and third parties working on behalf of MnDOT. It says private, confidential, protected, or otherwise not public data must not be entered into public GenAI tools. It also says GenAI should be supplemental, not a replacement for human judgment, and AI output used for decision-making must be verified for accuracy, relevance, and factuality.

Source: Minnesota DOT, “Generative Artificial Intelligence — Standards.”

Case 8 — Risk Management Framework: NIST AI RMF

NIST AI Risk Management Framework is not a transportation-specific regulation, and it is voluntary, but it is a major federal reference framework for managing AI risk. It is useful when explaining why project teams should think in terms of trustworthiness, governance, risk mapping, measurement, and monitoring.

Source: NIST, “AI Risk Management Framework.”

Practical takeaway for consultants

Use only client-approved and company-approved AI tools. Do not upload confidential, sensitive, security-related, PII, or nonpublic client data into public AI systems. Keep humans in the loop. Verify AI outputs, citations, numbers, assumptions, and sources. For government-client deliverables, treat AI as drafting, research, QA/QC, and workflow support — not as the final authority.

---

content.content: Industry-Specific Opportunities

AI can improve the workflows around transportation planning and engineering tools.
Where AI Can Help
• Literature reviews and technical scans• Project documentation and meeting summaries• Model documentation and version tracking• Scenario planning support• QA/QC checks and consistency reviews• Dashboard and visualization prototyping• Asset risk and resilience analysis support• Internal knowledge management• Training and onboarding junior staff

Important Distinction
AI may not replace specialized tools like travel demand models, GIS, Synchro, HCS, or design software.
But it can make the work around those tools much faster: preparing inputs, reviewing outputs, documenting assumptions, and communicating results.
Key Message
AI does not replace the technical workflow. It makes the workflow easier to manage, review, and explain.
EXPANDED CONTENT DRAFT • content first; structure and design later

content.content: Will AI Replace My Job?

What Still Needs Humans
• Professional judgment• Local context• Client communication• Assumption review• QA/QC• Ethical and legal accountability• Final decisions and recommendations
Human-in-the-Loop Role
AI can draft, organize, search, summarize, calculate, and prototype.
Humans define the problem, verify the sources, interpret the results, and take responsibility for the final deliverable.

Main Message
AI will replace some tasks, but not the full professional role — especially in transportation planning and engineering.
Tasks AI Can Help Automate
• Routine writing and summaries• Basic data cleaning• Standard report sections• Simple coding and scripting• Initial research and document review
EXPANDED CONTENT DRAFT • content first; structure and design later

notes:
Source: ai_ppt_full_content_dump_numbered.md — Page 22

## Page 22 — Will AI Replace My Job?
[Original label: Slide X — Will AI Replace My Job?]
Main message:
 AI will replace some tasks, but not the full professional role — at least not in transportation planning and engineering today.
Concerns:
Routine writing may be automated
Basic summaries may be automated
Simple data cleaning may be automated
Standard report sections may be automated
Basic coding and scripting may be automated
Junior-level research tasks may become faster and cheaper
What still needs humans:
Professional judgment
Local context
Client communication
Engineering responsibility
Assumption review
QA/QC
Ethical and legal accountability
Final decisions and recommendations
Human-in-the-loop role:
 AI can draft, organize, search, summarize, calculate, and prototype.
 Humans still define the problem, check the assumptions, verify the sources, interpret the results, and take responsibility for the final deliverable.
Opportunities for consultants:
Produce faster first drafts
Review more documents in less time
Build better internal knowledge bases
Prototype dashboards and tools faster
Learn new technical skills faster
Automate repetitive workflows
Support more projects with the same team
Move from manual production work toward higher-value advisory work
Key takeaway:
 AI may not replace transportation professionals directly, but professionals who use AI well will outperform those who do not.

content.content: Opportunities for Consultants and Future Trends

AI can help consultants work faster, reduce repetitive production work, and focus more on higher-value advisory tasks.
Consulting Opportunities
• Faster first drafts and document reviews• Better internal knowledge bases• Automated repetitive workflows• Faster dashboard and tool prototyping• Faster technical learning• Support for more projects with the same team
Future Trends
• AI embedded in Office, GIS, modeling platforms, and project management tools• More multimodal AI: text, maps, images, tables, audio, and video together• More RAG-based internal knowledge systems• Better integration with enterprise data systems• More formal AI governance from companies and agencies• AI-assisted coding, reporting, and dashboard generation

• AI-assisted scenario analysis• AI-supported resilience and risk screening• Natural language access to transportation datasets• Faster model QA/QC and documentation• Better public-facing communication tools

AI may not replace transportation professionals directly, but professionals who use AI well will outperform those who do not.

content.content: My View: How We Should Use AI in Our Industry

My View: How We Should Use AI in Our Industry
AI is a workflow multiplier, not a standalone expert.
Transportation work depends on data, assumptions, local context, engineering judgment, and stakeholder coordination. AI can help organize, accelerate, and explain this work, but it should be used carefully, practically, and transparently.
Recommended Next Steps
• Start with low-risk internal tasks• Use approved tools only• Verify every important output• Build reusable prompt templates and examples• Identify repeatable workflows, not just one-time experiments• Treat AI as part of quality-controlled project delivery

Final Message
AI will not replace transportation professionals.
But transportation professionals who understand both the technical domain and how to use AI will have a major advantage.
The strongest users will be domain experts who can prompt, verify, and operationalize AI.
EXPANDED CONTENT DRAFT • content first; structure and design later

notes:
Source: ai_ppt_full_content_dump_numbered.md — Page 21

## Page 21 — My View: Where AI Fits in Our Industry
[Original label: Slide 14 — My View: Where AI Fits in Our Industry]
AI is a workflow multiplier, not a standalone expert
Core viewpoint:
Transportation work depends on data, assumptions, local context, engineering judgment, and stakeholder coordination
AI can help organize, accelerate, and explain this work
The biggest value is not replacing technical analysis; it is reducing friction around analysis
Personal take:
AI will reward people who understand both the technical domain and how to structure AI-assisted workflows
The strongest users will be “domain experts who can prompt, verify, and operationalize AI”
